coredata.dc:title: Designing an electrochemical aptasensor based on immobilization of the aptamer onto nanocomposite for detection of the streptomycin antibiotic
coredata.dc:creator: Roushani, Mahmoud
Ghanbari, Kazhal
Jafar Hoseini, S.
coredata.dc:description: Streptomycin (STR), an aminoglycoside antibiotic, was used in human and veterinary to treat gram-negative infections. STR residue causes serious side effects on human health. Accordingly, to achieve a novel and sensitive method for detection of STR, we attempted to develop electrochemical sensing system based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) functionalized with amine (NH) and thiol (SH) groups (GQDs-N-S) as the promising newest carbon-based nanomaterial. In this sensing system, gold nanoparticle (AuNPs) was deposited on the GCE surface. Then GQDs-N-S was coated on the AuNPs/GCE surface. After, the silver nanoparticle (AgNPs) coated on the GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE. Finally, the STR thiol-aptamer (SH-Apt) was covalently attached on the AgNPs/GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE via bonding formation of AgS. This system showed linear range from 0.01 pg·mL−1 to 812.21 pg·mL−1 with limit of detection of 0.0033 pg·mL−1; it was as good as or better than other similar assays, and also the proposed system, in the presence of different interferences, displayed good selectivity in detecting STR. The proposed aptasensor showed excellent results in quantitatively the detection of STR in serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Streptomycin
Aptamer
Graphene quantum dots
Gold nanoparticle
Silver nanoparticle

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the deposited AuNPs on the GCE
Electrochemical characterization of the stepwise modification process
Analytical application of the STR aptasensor
Stability, reproducibility and selectivity study of the fabricated aptasensor
Application of the aptasensor in real sample analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study has been supported by the Ilam University . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) UV–Vis spectra of the synthesized AgNPs.
A; (a and b) FE-SEM images of GQDs-N-S, B; (a) EDAX spectrum and (b-g) elemental mapping of GQDs-N-S and C; XRD spectra of GQDs-N-S.

Nyquist curves of the recorded modified electrode for each steps in: GCE (curve a), AuNPs/GCE (curve b), GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE (curve c), AgNPs/GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE (curve d), Apt/AgNPs/GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE (curve e), BSA/Apt/AgNPs/GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE (curve f). Inset is the equivalent circuit. At the frequency range of 0.1 Hz–10 kHz.

Cyclic voltammograms of different electrodes in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−; scan rate, 100 mV s−1 GCE (curve a), AuNPs/GCE (curve b), GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE (curve c), AgNPs/GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE (curve d), Apt/AgNPs/GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE (curve e), BSA/Apt/AgNPs/GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE (curve f).

A) EISs obtained after incubating with different concentrations of STR (0.0, 0.01, 0.11, 0.21, 0.71, 1.21, 2.21, 12.21, 112.21, 312.21 and 812.21 pg·mL−1) in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. And B) calibration curve of the sensor for detecting STR.
The response peak intensity for 5 electrodes which modified under the same conditions.

Bar chart of the changes of the aptasensor response after incubation with interferences such as amoxicillin, ciprofloxacin and gentamicin at the concentration of 1 pg·mL−1 for each interferences in the presence of 10 fg·mL−1 (0.01 pg·mL−1) of STR.
Schematic illustration of the proposed electrochemical aptasensor for streptomycin detection.
Electrochemical detection of STR in serum samples.
Analytical parameters for aptasensors have been recently used for streptomycin detection.

head.ce:title: Designing an electrochemical aptasensor based on immobilization of the aptamer onto nanocomposite for detection of the streptomycin antibiotic

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Ilam University, Ilam, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 7194684795, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Streptomycin (STR), an aminoglycoside antibiotic, was used in human and veterinary to treat gram-negative infections. STR residue causes serious side effects on human health. Accordingly, to achieve a novel and sensitive method for detection of STR, we attempted to develop electrochemical sensing system based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) functionalized with amine (NH) and thiol (SH) groups (GQDs-N-S) as the promising newest carbon-based nanomaterial. In this sensing system, gold nanoparticle (AuNPs) was deposited on the GCE surface. Then GQDs-N-S was coated on the AuNPs/GCE surface. After, the silver nanoparticle (AgNPs) coated on the GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE. Finally, the STR thiol-aptamer (SH-Apt) was covalently attached on the AgNPs/GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE via bonding formation of AgS. This system showed linear range from 0.01 pg·mL−1 to 812.21 pg·mL−1 with limit of detection of 0.0033 pg·mL−1; it was as good as or better than other similar assays, and also the proposed system, in the presence of different interferences, displayed good selectivity in detecting STR. The proposed aptasensor showed excellent results in quantitatively the detection of STR in serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Streptomycin (STR) is a member of aminoglycoside purified from Streptomyces that is used in human and veterinary to treat gram-negative infections [1–3]. The presence of these residues even in minute amount can trigger potential health hazard such as nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity [4–6]. Thus, development of sensitive methods for the detection of STR residue in serum and animal derived foods are in great demand both in the field of environmental analysis and food safety control. A variety of analytical approaches have been developed for STR determination such as: high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7,8], Liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [9,10], immunoassays including radio immunoassay (RIA), fluorescence immunoassay and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [5,12,13]. HPLC has high sensitivity but it needs post-column derivatization due to lack of chromophore group in STR. LC-MS has excellent sensitivity for the detection of STR but it is labor-intensive and expensive. Immunoassays such as RIA, FIA and ELISA have cross-reactions with other materials in biological sample analysis. Several analytical approaches on applying aptamers have been introduced for the detection of STR accurately [14–16]. Aptamers are short single strand DNA (ssDNAs)/RNA molecules that bind diverse targets with high affinity and specificity [17,18]. Aptamers have some advantages in comparison with other sensing methods, including low cost, simplicity of synthesis and modification, excellent thermal stability, lack of toxicity and immunogenicity, high sensitivity and rapid response. Because of these unique properties, aptamers have gained potential application in fabrication of biosensors [19,20]. This study focused on aptamers as recognition elements in nanosensing platforms. In the past few years, different nanoparticles (NPs) such as gold NPs, platinum NPs, silver NPs and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been developed to improve the sensitivity and performance of electrochemical immunosensor and aptasensor [21–27]. GQDs are one or few-layered graphene sheets with lateral size smaller than 100 nm [28–30]. GQDs are demonstrated to be efficient in the field of electrochemical sensors and biosensors, because of their chemical stability, suitable conductivity, good biocompatibility and low-toxicity [31,32]. The silver NPs have several advantages including strong adsorption ability, simplicity of preparation process, cost-effective manufacturing method, good conductivity and large specific surface area [33,34], that have been developed to improve the sensitivity and performance of electrochemical immunosensor and aptasensors [35,36].

This paper reports a novel and highly selective aptasensor on the GC electrode surface for the ultrasensitive detection of STR. In the present study, AuNPs, GQDs-N-S and AgNPs, as efficient nanocomposite, were used for the immobilization of Apt. At the first stage, AuNPs, via the deposition–precipitation (DP) method, were deposited on the GC electrode surface. Then GQDs-N-S were simply coated on the GC electrode surface by the drop casting method, the thiol-group and some of the amino groups of the GQDs-N-S was covalently attached on the AuNPs. At the next stage, the AgNPs coated on the electrode modified with GQDs-N-S, the amine-group of the GQDs-N-S was covalently attached on the AgNPs. In the proposed sensing strategy, the STR thiol-aptamer (SH-Apt), via bonding formation of AgS, was covalently attached on the AgNPs, which in turn, were attached on the surface of GC electrode modified by the GQDs-N-S. By incubation STR molecule as a target, the Apt/STR complex was formed on the electrode surface and the electron transfer (eT) characteristic of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, as the electrochemical probe, was changed. To the best of our knowledge, an application of BSA/Apt/AgNPs/GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE and [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, a probe for the detection of STR, has never been reported yet. The most obvious advantages of the proposed system compared to previous reports, despite its simplicity, low cost, and speed response, is the ability to detect STR at pg·mL−1 concentration. Whereas, in the previous reports, STR has been determined in the range of ng·mL−1 and nanomolar.

The sequence of the streptomycin aptamer was as follows [16]: 5′-TAG GGA ATT CGT CGA CGG ATC CGG GGT CTG GTG TTC TGC TTT GTT CTG TCG GGT CGT CTG CAG GTC GAC GCA TGC GCC G-Thiol-3′ (Bioneer Co, South Korea). All chemicals used in the study were of analytical reagent grade purity. Streptomycin, trisodium citrate dehydrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O), AgNO3, sodium borohydride, (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane, (3-mercaptopropyl) triethoxysilane, citric acid, tetrachloroauric acid (HAuCl4) and all other reagents ordered from commercial suppliers (Sigma-Aldrich, Merck or Fluka Company). Cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed with a μ-AUTOLAB electrochemical system type III in conjunction FRA2 board computer controlled Potentiostat/Galvanostat (Eco-Chemio, Switzerland) driven with NOVA software. The working electrode was a modified GCE, and the auxiliary and reference electrodes were a platinum wire and an Ag/AgCl (satd 3.0 M KCl), respectively. The surface morphology and spectroscopic characterizations of the samples were studied by using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Model: Hitachi S4160). The samples were sputter coated with gold before observation and then were mounted in SEM and scanned at the desired magnification. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigations were carried out using an electron microscope (Hitachi H-800). Spectra of energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was obtained using a Vega-Tescan electron microscope and a minimal amount of sample was placed on its carbon tape. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were recorded with a Carry Eclipse (Agilent). Raman spectra were recorded from 4250 cm−1 to 100 cm−1 on a high resolution dispersive Raman Thermonicolet (LabRAM HR UV/Vis/NIR, Electrooptics).

GCE was polished down to mirror-like status with alumina powder and then sonicated in a mixture of water and ethanol (1:1) for 5 min. After it dried at ambient temperature, the AuNPs/GCE surface was achieved by the DP method using a solution containing 5 mM HAuCl4, as the gold precursor, and 0.5 M H2SO4. By applying a potential at −0.2 V for 200 s, AuNPs were electrochemistry deposited on the highly polished GCE. To prove that the AuNPs are deposited on the surface of the GCE, the SEM image was recorded.

GQDs were synthesized according to the reported procedure [31]. In brief, a 5 mL beaker contain 2 g of citric acid heated up to 200 °C using a heating mantle. By passing a time (about 5 min), the citric acid became liquid followed by the color change from colorless to pale yellow and then orange in about 30 min. The obtained orange liquid was added dropwise into the NaOH aqueous solution (10 mg mL−1, 100 mL) on the stirrer. GQDs were obtained when the pH neutralized to 7.0.

In order to functionalize the GQDs with amine and thiol groups, GQDs solution was heated to evaporate the solvent. The obtained solid (172 mg) was placed in a clean and dry round-bottom flask. Then, 30 mL of dry toluene were added and the mixture was placed in a sonic bath for 20 min. The (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (1.79 mL) and (3-mercaptopropyl) triethoxysilane (1.79 mL) were added to the GQDs from a microsyringe in a nitrogen atmosphere. The mixture was refluxed for 12 h and finally was cooled down to room temperature. The pale yellow product was obtained from the reaction mixture and washed with dry toluene. The product was dried under vacuum for one day at ambient temperature. To prove that the GQDs-N-S are synthesized, the SEM and TEM images, the EDS and the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra, Raman and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded.

AgNPs were synthesized based on a previously described method [34]. In a typical procedure, 20 mL of 1.0 mM AgNO3 aqueous solution, 0.5 mL of 0.1 M sodium citrate solutions and 1 mL of 3.5 mM NaBH4 were prepared. The prepared solutions were added dropwise to 200 mL boiled water which had been cooled. Then, it was stirred vigorously for 2 h and a transparent yellow homogenous colloidal solution of AgNPs was obtained. The synthetic AgNPs characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy and TEM.

AuNPs were electrochemistry deposited on the surface of the polished GCE. The AuNPs/GCE were covered by 10 μL of the GQDs-N-S solution and dried in the room temperature for 3 h, the thiol group and some of the amine groups of GQDs-N-S were attached to AuNPs via bonding formation of Au-S and Au-N. Subsequently, the solution of AgNPs was dropped onto the GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE surface. After 4 h, AgNPs were attached on amine groups of the GQDs-N-S. Afterwards, a 10 μL Apt (2 μM) was dropped on the surface of the AgNPs/GQDs-N-S/AuNPs/GCE surface for overnight (at 4 °C) in order to immobilize the Apt molecules on the surface of AgNPs by chemisorption between AgNPs and thiol groups of Apt. Then 10 μL of BSA (1%) solution was used to cover the surface of electrode to reduce the effective surface area and available active sites and avoid non-specific adsorption. The resulting modified electrode was washed with PBS three times to eliminate the unbounded substance and stored at 4 °C before use. By incubation of different STR concentrations, the Apt/STR complex was formed on the modified electrode surface. Changes of the electrochemical signal were monitored by EIS as the measuring technique. The fabrication process, stability and selectivity of the proposed nanoaptasensor were studied by CV, DPV and EIS as the effective electrochemical techniques. Scheme 1 shows the schematic representation for the fabrication of the nanoaptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the deposited AuNPs on the GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the AuNPs electrodeposited on the surface of the bare GCE were characterized with SEM image. As shown in Fig. S1 (AuNPs deposited on the GCE), it can be seen that the AuNPs layer, as a uniform layer, was successfully deposited.

The morphology of the spherical AgNPs was investigated with TEM image (Fig. 1A). The diameter of these NPs has a size range from 4 nm to around 20 nm with the average size of ca. 10 nm. As shown in Fig. 1B, The existence of an absorption peak at 392 nm confirms the successful synthesis of AgNPs. Determination of the size and concentration of the as-prepared AgNPs can be inferred from the UV–Vis spectra [37].

Fig. 2A (a and b) shows the field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) image of GQDSs-N-S prepared through the procedure mentioned above. As it can be seen, the formed GQDSs-N-S have a diameter of approximately 400 nm to 2.5 μm. The energy dispersive analysis of X-ray (EDS) and elemental mapping exhibits the presence of C, N, O, Si and S elements in GQDs-N-S (Fig. 2B; a–g). GQDs-N-S was also characterized using the XRD. Fig. 2C shows the XRD patterns. Fig. S2 shows a TEM image of the as-synthesized GQDs-N-S with an approximately mean diameter of 420 nm. Raman spectrum (Fig. S3) exhibits graphene structure for the as-synthesized GQDs. The peaks at 1330 and 1571 cm−1 are related to the D and G bands, respectively. The ID/IG ratio is lower in the case of GQDs (0.84) than of graphene oxide (0.90) and reduced-graphene oxide (1.06) [38] which is the result of the large amount of oxygen groups on the GQDs edges. Fig. S4 shows the PL spectra of the GQDs-N-S which is in consistent with previously reported N-GQDs and GQDs PL spectra [38–40]. All performed tests in this section can be a confirmation of the successful synthesis of the GQDs-N-S.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the stepwise modification process
ce:section.ce:para: EIS has been performed to characterize the GC electrode interface during each modification steps, and Randles equivalent circuit [Rs(Cdl [RctW])] was used to fit the experimental data. Where RS is the solution resistance, Rct is the electron-transfer resistance, Cdl is the double layer capacitance and Zw is the Warburg impedance, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3 . The change in the values of charge transfer resistance (Rct) were observed upon the stepwise formation of the modified electrode. EIS plots (Fig. 3) obtained at a potential of 0.25 V, Initially it can be seen that for AuNPs/GCE (curve b) the value Rct decreased compared to the bare GCE (curve a) due to the higher eT ability and enlarged the active surface area of the deposited neatly arranged AuNPs. After casting some of GQD-N-S on the surface of AuNPs/GCE, Rct value decreased compared to AuNPs/GCE (curve c). The mechanism that can be proposed to describe the interaction of GQD-N-S with the deposited AuNPs is shown in Fig. S5. The gold atoms interacted with the thiol group via bonding formation of AuS, and also interacted with some of the N atoms of the amino group so that the gold atoms were chelated by the two adjacent amine or thiol groups [41]. Therefore, it led to positive partial charge of thiol and amine groups on the GQD-N-S nanomaterial. So, the presence of the positive partial charge on the GCE surface reduced the repulsion of the probe and eT increasing. In the next step, when AgNPs were dropped on the surface of the electrode, Rct value increased (curve d). AgNPs with negative charge immobilized onto the NH groups of GQD-N-S via bonding formation of AgN, it led to the repulsion between [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− anions and the AgNPs to the extent that the response of redox probe was reduced and thereby the Rct value was increased. In the next step, as expected according to curve e, Rct was increased by loading the Apt on the electrode surface. This increase in value indicated that the covalent attachment of the thiol-Apt onto the AgNPs surface took place via the formation of a favored AgS bond between the thiol group of the Apt and the AgNPs. Due to the negative charges of the phosphate groups in the Apt molecules, the eT of the redox probe was prevented and the extent of the probe response was reduced. The similar increasing also could be observed when BSA solution was dropped onto the surface of electrode (curve f) that can be attributed to the reducing of available active sites and the effective surface area for electron transfer process by BSA. As a result, the proposed aptasensor has been constructed successfully. CV technique is a favored technique to study layer by layer immobilization of the modified electrodes. Fig. 4 shows the typical CV responses of the modified electrodes in each step in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− which includes KCl (0.1 M). A reversible redox peaks at high current levels were observed at the bare GCE (Fig. 4, curve a), demonstrating the conducting nature of the bare electrode. Fig. 4, curve b showed a couple of better-defined redox peaks compared with the bare electrode. So it indicated that the deposited layer of AuNPs as a unique platform due to the higher eT ability and enlarged the active surface area could increase the current signal significantly. When the GQD-N-S was dropped onto the surface of AuNPs/GCE, the gold atoms formed a chelate by interaction with the thiol groups and some amine groups via bonding formation of AuS and AuN, so that it led to positive partial charge of thiol and amine groups on the GQD-N-S nanomaterial. Therefore, the peak current increased which was attributed to reduce repelling between positive partial charge of GQD-N-S and the redox probe anions. Thus, this phenomena led to increasing eT between redox probe and the electrode surface (Fig. 4, curve c). In the next step, when the AgNPs were immobilized onto the amine groups of the GQD-N-S, which formed a self-assemble monolayer via AgN bonds, because of the negative charge prevented eT of probe, an increase in the peak potential separation accrued to the decrease of the peak current (Fig. 4, curve d). After immobilization of Apt onto the electrode surface, formed a cluster of long spacer arms on the platform, resulting in more blocking of the electron flow of probe to the electrode surface and obvious decrease of the peak current and a shift in peak voltage were observed (Fig. 4, curve e). This changes implied that the electron transfer became more difficult that could be attributed to the repulsion between negative charges of aptamer phosphate groups and the redox probe anions, indicating that Apt was successfully immobilized on the electrode surface. Finally, the CV response decreased with the dropping of BSA solution onto the electrode surface (Fig. 4, curve f). This was attributed to reducing available active sites for electron transfer process by protein.

In order to provide an aptasensor with the best performance, the pH value of solution and the incubation time as important parameters for a high electrochemical response, should be optimized. Because the Apt is a biological molecular, chemical environment must be such that the Apt will not be destroyed. Thus, a neutral environment is essential. So, all of the experiments were carried out in a PB solution with pH of 7.4. The incubation time was investigated. It was found that the DPV peak current intensity decreased as the incubation time increased from 30 to 180 min, and it reached a minimum value at 120 min. Thus, 120 min was chosen as the optimum incubation time (Fig. S6). This value is in accordance with the literature [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application of the STR aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The dependence of electrochemical signals and the STR concentration was then investigated. Fig. 5 shows typical EIS of the aptasensor in the presence of different concentrations of STR (0.01–812.21 pg· mL−1). As shown in inset of Fig. 5, the ΔR have a good linear relationship versus the logarithm of the concentration of STR. The fitting equation is y = 1.9655x + 10.363 (R2 = 0.9908), in which y is the ΔR (kΏ) and x is the logarithm of the concentration of STR (pg· mL−1). LOD is calculated to be 0.0033 pg· mL−1(S/N = 3), which is quite low compared with current biosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and selectivity study of the fabricated aptasensor
Application of the aptasensor in real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the applicability of the developed aptasensor, we have spiked specific amounts of STR in serum samples, which are then tested by the proposed method. The results are listed in Table 1 . These results verified that this method may have practical utility in biological samples.

In this study, we built a novel and highly sensitive aptasensor utilizing AgNPs/GQDs-N-S/AuNPs nanocomposite coated on the GCE surface as a promising platform for the detection of striptomycin. AuNPs were electrochemistry deposited onto the GCE surface. Then GQDs-N-S was successfully synthesized and used to modify the GCE modified with AuNPs, via the interaction between SH2 group and some of the NH group of GQDs-N-S and AuNPs. AgNPs have been immobilized onto the NH groups of GQDs-N-S via bonding formation of AgN and aptamer have been loaded on the electrode surface via the interaction between SH2 group of the aptamer and AgNPs. The results suggested that the aptasensor can be used for the detection of STR at low concentration range pg·mL−1 and the method could successfully distinguish STR from other interferences with similar chemical structures. Moreover, it also performs well in serum samples. As presented in Table 2 , the advantages of this method was superior to that of the other methods reported in the related literature, especially in comparison to different characteristics such as simplicity, sensitivity, low-cost and the detection limit, which might become a powerful tool for the detection of other targets.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study has been supported by the Ilam University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2018.05.016.
